Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic most of the time. I'd plan to study 34 hours a week, and I did put in those hours, but it was rarely organized. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd read through everything, take detailed notes, and feel like I was making progress. Then life would happen, and I'd fall behind on actually attending classes or submitting work. I preferred reading and writing things out by hand, which took time but helped me understand better.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on my anxiety levels and what else was going on. Some weeks I'd study at consistent times, usually late at night when things were quiet. But when deadlines piled up or I started feeling overwhelmed, everything became reactive rather than planned. I'd sometimes study for days straight, then crash and avoid everything for a while. The regularity I wanted never really materialized.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember there was something due or worry that I'd missed an announcement. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me back in because they felt less intimidating than the regular coursework. I logged in frequently—probably 49 times over the course—but a lot of those were anxious check-ins rather than productive sessions.
Q4: For a brief period, checking the discussion forums became routine for me. I'd post and interact with peers, which felt manageable and less scary than tackling assignments. The gamified elements helped too—earning points or badges gave me small wins when everything else felt impossible. But that routine broke down when my anxiety spiked and I started avoiding the platform altogether.
Q5: The discussion forums and peer interactions were genuinely helpful because they made me feel less alone. I posted 16 times and had 34 peer interactions, which might not sound like much, but those conversations helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The videos were useful too—I watched over 180 minutes of them—because I could pause and take notes in my own style. Reading materials that I could download and annotate were my favorite.
Q6: I ignored the attendance-based activities almost entirely—my attendance was barely 2%. Live sessions made my anxiety spike because I couldn't control the pace or go back over things I missed. I also delayed assignments constantly even though I knew how to do them well. My assignment scores were high when I actually submitted, but the completion rate was terrible because I'd freeze up before clicking submit.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see clear learning value and approach it at my own pace. The gamified tasks worked because they broke things into smaller, less threatening pieces. I needed to feel like I could succeed before I'd invest effort. If something felt too open-ended or high-stakes, I'd convince myself I needed more preparation and never actually start.
Q8: Optional activities felt safer, paradoxically. I engaged more with discussion posts and peer interactions because failing at them didn't count against my grade. Required activities triggered this perfectionism-anxiety spiral where I'd prepare extensively but then not submit. The stakes made everything harder. Optional things let me learn without the fear of judgment.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and PDFs, print them out, and annotate them extensively. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries and concept maps that nobody would ever see in the LMS. I also used external websites and YouTube videos to supplement what I didn't understand from the course materials. My read-write learning style meant I needed to physically write things to remember them.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with the material but never logged in. I'd be at my desk with printed readings, textbooks, and my notes spread everywhere, genuinely learning. But the LMS would show nothing. Sometimes I'd watch a video once, take screenshots, and study from those for days. My offline engagement was probably triple what the system could track.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or video once, download or screenshot everything I needed, and then work with it offline for extended periods. This was my preferred method because it reduced my anxiety about internet issues and let me study in my own environment without the pressure of being 'watched' by the system. One download could fuel a week of studying.
Q12: There were several points where engaging became nearly impossible. When multiple deadlines converged, my anxiety would spike to the point of paralysis. I'd know exactly what needed to be done but couldn't make myself do it. The gap between my preparation and my actual submissions grew wider. Around midterms, I basically shut down despite having all the knowledge I needed.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing overdue assignments made everything worse. Fatigue from those intense study sessions left me depleted. I had the resources and time, but my mental state created barriers that had nothing to do with the material difficulty. The workload felt manageable on paper but impossible in my head.
Q14: Many times I'd log in, click through materials, and realize I'd absorbed nothing. I was going through the motions to feel productive without actually engaging. Sometimes I'd watch videos while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about deadlines or catastrophizing about my grades. The engagement looked real in the data but felt hollow to me.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important and even when I knew I could do them well. My assignment scores were over 95% when I submitted, but I only completed about 2% of them. The perfectionism and anxiety created this impossible standard where I'd rather not submit than submit something imperfect. I'd tell myself I needed just a little more preparation, and then the deadline would pass.
Q16: Deadlines created crisis mode for me. I'd ignore things until the last possible moment, then either submit something rushed or miss it entirely. The gamified tasks helped because they had smaller, more frequent deadlines that felt less catastrophic. But major deadlines triggered avoidance. I'd spend the time before a deadline preparing extensively, then freeze when it came time to actually submit.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Positive feedback on the work I did submit was encouraging, but it also highlighted how much I was leaving on the table. Knowing I could score 95% on assignments made my failure to submit them even more frustrating. The gamified feedback—points and badges—was easier to handle because it felt lower stakes. Critical feedback sometimes sent me into avoidance spirals.
Q18: The course structure with its flexibility was both helpful and harmful for me. Having access to materials anytime meant I could study in my preferred way, but it also meant no external structure forced me to stay on track. The gamified elements provided some structure that worked for my brain. But overall, I needed more scaffolding than the course provided to actually complete things rather than just learn them.
Q19: The online format made engagement harder because it required self-direction I struggled to maintain with my anxiety. The gamified tasks made things easier by breaking work into smaller pieces with immediate feedback. Discussion forums helped because they were lower pressure. But the big assignments with single deadlines were almost impossible for me to approach. More frequent, smaller submissions would have helped enormously.
Q20: That describes me exactly. I was highly active—logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions, engaging with gamified elements—but my actual submissions were almost nonexistent. The activity represents genuine learning and engagement with the material. The missing submissions represent anxiety and perfectionism blocking the final step. Someone can be deeply engaged with content while being paralyzed about producing output.
Q21: That's the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some students are efficient—they engage briefly but effectively, then perform well. They don't need the extensive preparation and repetition I do. Or they're learning through other means entirely. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low engagement; it might mean different engagement. My high activity didn't translate to performance either, just differently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, time spent, and participation in trackable activities. They capture the digital footprint accurately. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline learning, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't see that I watched a video three times because of anxiety rather than confusion. They can't measure the notebooks full of notes or the understanding I developed but couldn't demonstrate.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, wanting to understand it, and making it part of your thinking. For me, I was deeply engaged with the content—I spent 34 hours a week on it, I took extensive notes, I participated in discussions. But engagement also means completing the cycle by producing work and attending, which I failed at. True engagement should include both the learning and the demonstration of learning.
Q24: The LMS data completely misses my anxiety and how it shaped everything. It can't show that I knew the material well enough to score 95% but couldn't bring myself to submit. It misses the hours of offline studying, the notebooks, the genuine understanding I developed. It can't capture that my low attendance and completion weren't about not caring or not understanding—they were about being paralyzed by perfectionism and fear. My crisis wasn't about capability; it was about execution.
